conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have three children - Alex who's 10, Blake who's 19, and Charlie who's just 2 years old. I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know about Alex (10), Blake (19), and Charlie (2).

Let me ask you some questions to determine eligibility for each of them. I'll start with some general questions that apply to all three children.

First, do all three children normally live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, I live in Birmingham, so yes I'm in the UK. Alex and Blake both live at home with me. Charlie's situation is a bit different though - Charlie doesn't live with me, actually. I'm fostering Charlie and the little one has been in hospital for a couple of months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand that Alex and Blake live with you in Birmingham, and Charlie is a foster child who has been in hospital for a couple of months.

Let me gather a bit more information about each child's situation:

Starting with Blake who is 19 - is Blake currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs, or similar non-advanced qualifications)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in any kind of education at the moment. Finished school a while back and is just figuring things out right now - not studying or doing any training courses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that about Blake.

Now, regarding Charlie who you're fostering and is currently in hospital - are you receiving any fostering allowance or payments from the local authority for fostering Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't get anything from the council for fostering Charlie. No maintenance payments or anything like that from them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Regarding Charlie being in hospital - has Charlie been in hospital continuously, or have there been breaks where Charlie came home? And do you expect Charlie to return to live with you when discharged?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie's been in hospital continuously for about two months now - a health issue that needed proper treatment. As for when Charlie gets out, well, Charlie doesn't actually live with me. I'm fostering, but the little one lives elsewhere normally. I haven't really been able to contribute financially towards Charlie's care either, to be honest.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So to make sure I understand Charlie's situation correctly: Charlie is a foster child who normally lives elsewhere (not with you), and you haven't been contributing financially to Charlie's upbringing. Is that right?

And just to clarify - who does Charlie normally live with when not in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I haven't been contributing anything financially towards Charlie's care. To be honest, I'm not entirely sure about the exact living arrangements for Charlie outside of the fostering situation. It's a bit complicated. But you're correct that Charlie doesn't normally live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me now ask about Alex, who is 10 and lives with you. 

Is there anyone else who might also be claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or who might have an equal or greater claim to Child Benefit for Alex (such as another parent or guardian that Alex also lives with or spends significant time with)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me - Alex lives with me full time and there's no one else making a claim for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And regarding Blake, who is 19 and lives with you - is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or does anyone else have an equal or greater claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, same situation really - Blake lives with me and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Just me here.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Before I make my final assessment, let me just read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information about your eligibility for each child.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. Based on all the information you've provided and the official Child Benefit rules, I can now provide you with a full assessment of your eligibility for each of your three children.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

Based on the information you've provided, here's the outcome for each of your children:

**✓ Alex (age 10) - ELIGIBLE**
You can claim Child Benefit for Alex. Since Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming for them, you meet all the standard eligibility requirements.

**✗ Blake (age 19) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake. While Child Benefit can continue for young people aged 16-19, they must be in approved education or training. Since Blake has finished school and is not currently in any education or training, they don't meet the eligibility criteria.

**✗ Charlie (age 2) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie. Although you're fostering Charlie, the eligibility rules require you to be "responsible" for the child, which means either living with them or contributing financially towards their care (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount). Since Charlie doesn't normally live with you and you haven't been contributing financially to their upkeep, you're not considered responsible for Charlie under Child Benefit rules.

**Next Steps:**
You should apply for Child Benefit for Alex. If Blake returns to approved education or training in the future, you may be able to claim for them at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 2 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 8 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. You are responsible for Alex as you live with them. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Alex meets all the criteria for eligibility.

Blake is 19 years old and is not in approved education or training. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 only if they stay in approved education or training. Since Blake finished school and is not currently in any education or training, Blake does not meet the criteria for Child Benefit eligibility.

Charlie is a foster child who does not normally live with you, and you have not been contributing financially towards Charlie's care. According to the eligibility rules, you'll get Child Benefit if you foster a child as long as the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. However, you must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them or paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since Charlie doesn't live with you and you haven't been contributing financially, you are not considered responsible for Charlie under Child Benefit rules. Additionally, even though Charlie has been in hospital for 2 months, this doesn't change the fundamental issue that you don't have responsibility for Charlie's care. Therefore, Charlie does not meet the eligibility criteria.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children: Alex (10), Blake (19), and Charlie (2). They are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex only. Alex is under 16 and lives with them, meeting all standard eligibility criteria. Blake is 19 and not in approved education or training, so does not qualify. Charlie is a foster child who doesn't live with the claimant and for whom the claimant doesn't contribute financially, so the claimant is not considered responsible for Charlie and therefore cannot claim Child Benefit for them.
